attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	---
2	name: Revised build plan — DB-first, scootchain deferred, staking is core
3	description: Priority order: chat, staking ritual, Scoot token (Postgres-direct), scootchain integration later
4	type: project
5	originSessionId: fc4abb98-a71e-4272-ab69-d327858311ed
6	---
7	Scootchain (the C blockchain daemon) is the long-term goal but is NOT a prerequisite for a working app. Everything runs from Postgres directly until the chain is ready.
8	
9	## Priority order
10	
11	1. **Chat foundation** — rooms, messages, live WebSocket delivery (DONE — works end to end).
12	2. **Chat polish** — read receipts + unread counts, DMs, image attachments. These do not require staking and can ship first.
13	3. **Staking ritual (pledges)** — the in-person QR + code + selfie ceremony. See `social_graph_staking.md` for the full flow. This unlocks identity-aware features and is a prerequisite for stake-gated chat/wallet behavior.
14	4. **Scoot token** — balances, send/receive, transaction history, Postgres via Drizzle (no C daemon, no bridge yet). Likely tied to staking (Scoot as stake collateral, slashing on revocation).
15	5. **Room privacy / staking-gated chat** — visibility and DM rules expressed as graph predicates against the staking graph. Comes after both staking and token exist.
16	6. **Scootchain integration** — future phase; existing DB transactions and pledges get committed to the chain when ready.
17	
18	## Why this order
19	
20	- Chat works today and the user can use it. Read receipts/DMs/images are pure additive UX and don't depend on identity.
21	- Staking is the core identity primitive of the platform — every feature that's "user-to-user trust" depends on it. Build it before designing the trust-dependent features.
22	- Token comes after staking so the staking ↔ wallet relationship (collateral, slashing) is designed once, not twice.
23	- Room privacy is deferred until after staking + token because its design changes radically depending on what graph queries are available.
24	- Scootchain is last because the DB is the source of truth and will feed the chain later.
25	
26	## Practical implications
27	
28	- Wallet endpoints will use Drizzle directly, not the C bridge.
29	- Schema additions to plan for: `pledges` (staker_id, stakee_id, selfie_url, created_at, revoked_at), `pledge_codes` (one-time codes for the ritual), `wallets` and `transactions` later.
30	- Pledge selfies are private-to-staker by default (the selfie is the staker's memory aid for recognizing returning users — see `social_graph_staking.md` for full reasoning). Storage path needs access control, not just a different bucket.
31	- The C bridge and scootd daemon are deferred until chat, staking, and token are solid.
32

file.content:
---
name: Revised build plan — DB-first, scootchain deferred, staking is core
description: Priority order: chat, staking ritual, Scoot token (Postgres-direct), scootchain integration later
type: project
originSessionId: fc4abb98-a71e-4272-ab69-d327858311ed
---
Scootchain (the C blockchain daemon) is the long-term goal but is NOT a prerequisite for a working app. Everything runs from Postgres directly until the chain is ready.

## Priority order

1. **Chat foundation** — rooms, messages, live WebSocket delivery (DONE — works end to end).
2. **Chat polish** — read receipts + unread counts, DMs, image attachments. These do not require staking and can ship first.
3. **Staking ritual (pledges)** — the in-person QR + code + selfie ceremony. See `social_graph_staking.md` for the full flow. This unlocks identity-aware features and is a prerequisite for stake-gated chat/wallet behavior.
4. **Scoot token** — balances, send/receive, transaction history, Postgres via Drizzle (no C daemon, no bridge yet). Likely tied to staking (Scoot as stake collateral, slashing on revocation).
5. **Room privacy / staking-gated chat** — visibility and DM rules expressed as graph predicates against the staking graph. Comes after both staking and token exist.
6. **Scootchain integration** — future phase; existing DB transactions and pledges get committed to the chain when ready.

## Why this order

- Chat works today and the user can use it. Read receipts/DMs/images are pure additive UX and don't depend on identity.
- Staking is the core identity primitive of the platform — every feature that's "user-to-user trust" depends on it. Build it before designing the trust-dependent features.
- Token comes after staking so the staking ↔ wallet relationship (collateral, slashing) is designed once, not twice.
- Room privacy is deferred until after staking + token because its design changes radically depending on what graph queries are available.
- Scootchain is last because the DB is the source of truth and will feed the chain later.

## Practical implications

- Wallet endpoints will use Drizzle directly, not the C bridge.
- Schema additions to plan for: `pledges` (staker_id, stakee_id, selfie_url, created_at, revoked_at), `pledge_codes` (one-time codes for the ritual), `wallets` and `transactions` later.
- Pledge selfies are private-to-staker by default (the selfie is the staker's memory aid for recognizing returning users — see `social_graph_staking.md` for full reasoning). Storage path needs access control, not just a different bucket.
- The C bridge and scootd daemon are deferred until chat, staking, and token are solid.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: Chat bot architecture — multi-bot, table-driven, @mention triggered
3	description: How LLM bots are modeled in Scoot (multi-bot, personality per bot, @mention in chat), with key design decisions locked in
4	type: project
5	originSessionId: 30c264ac-2abc-4e83-9020-c83e0856f83f
6	---
7	Bots are first-class chat participants in Scoot, not a separate subsystem. Users invoke them via `@<botname>` in any chat message, and the bot replies in the same room as a regular message.
8	
9	## Identity
10	
11	A bot is a row in `users` with `is_bot = true`. This keeps the message/membership/broadcast model unchanged — bot messages are just messages.
12	
13	`users` has two added columns:
14	- `is_bot boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT false` — the marker
15	- `display_name text` (nullable) — opt-in display override. Falls back to `username`. **Applies to humans AND bots.** This is the resolution of the earlier "login handle vs alias" open question — yes, we have both, but `display_name` is optional, not enforced.
16	
17	## Per-bot config in `bots` table
18	
19	A new `bots` table holds personality + behavior per bot:
20	- `user_id` (PK, FK → users.id)
21	- `system_prompt text NOT NULL` — the personality. Edited via SQL or `bot:edit` CLI.
22	- `auto_join_new_rooms boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT false` — `true` only for the primary helpful assistant (`claude`). Personas (Kobe, Moses, etc.) are `false` and invited per-room.
23	- `enabled boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT true`
24	
25	## Mention semantics
26	
27	- Pattern: `@<username>` anywhere in a message, case-insensitive match against `users.username`
28	- First bot mention wins (multi-bot fanout deferred)
29	- Bots can't trigger bots — messages authored by `is_bot=true` users are skipped at the parser
30	- Bot must be a `roomMembers` row in the target room to be mentionable there
31	
32	## Dispatch flow
33	
34	1. Human sends message → route handler inserts + broadcasts + returns 201 immediately
35	2. Async: parse mentions, find matching bot member
36	3. Broadcast `{type: "typing", userId, username, roomId}` over WS
37	4. Build provider payload: last 20 messages with `"username: text"` prefix on user turns, plain content on bot turns, system prompt from `bots.system_prompt`
38	5. Call provider.chat() — Anthropic for v1, OpenAI-compat available via swap
39	6. Insert bot's reply as a `messages` row, broadcast as normal message
40	7. Broadcast `{type: "typing_stop", userId, roomId}`
41	8. On error: log via pino, post short "I'm having trouble responding…" message, broadcast stop
42	
43	## Locked decisions
44	
45	- **History window:** 20 messages of room history fed to the LLM per reply
46	- **Max reply tokens:** 500 (chat bubbles, not essays)
47	- **Provider for v1:** Anthropic only
48	- **Default bot:** `claude`, auto-joins new rooms, helpful/dry/direct system prompt
49	- **Personas:** added via `npm run bot:create` CLI, invited to rooms via `npm run bot:invite`
50	- **Typing indicator:** YES — both `typing` and `typing_stop` WS message types
51	- **Error visibility:** YES — user sees a graceful failure message in chat. Server logs everything via pino regardless.
52	- **UI scrollback:** unlimited, paginated 50 at a time. Server-side already supports `?before=`. Client-side scroll-to-load is a separate follow-up, NOT part of bot v1.
53	
54	## Bots and the staking graph
55	
56	Bots are not stakeable (per `social_graph_staking.md`). The `is_bot` flag is the explicit exclusion. Bots can be members of stake-gated rooms in the future; the gating predicate just doesn't apply to them.
57	
58	## Why not OpenClaw or similar agent platforms
59	
60	OpenClaw (openclaw.ai) is a personal AI assistant with shell/file/browser tools. Overkill — and a security risk — for a multi-user chat reply bot. The provider abstraction approach above gives `@<botname>` chat without exposing real-world action capability. Real agent integration (tools, actions) is a Phase 4+ concern, not v1.
61

file.content:
---
name: Chat bot architecture — multi-bot, table-driven, @mention triggered
description: How LLM bots are modeled in Scoot (multi-bot, personality per bot, @mention in chat), with key design decisions locked in
type: project
originSessionId: 30c264ac-2abc-4e83-9020-c83e0856f83f
---
Bots are first-class chat participants in Scoot, not a separate subsystem. Users invoke them via `@<botname>` in any chat message, and the bot replies in the same room as a regular message.

## Identity

A bot is a row in `users` with `is_bot = true`. This keeps the message/membership/broadcast model unchanged — bot messages are just messages.

`users` has two added columns:
- `is_bot boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT false` — the marker
- `display_name text` (nullable) — opt-in display override. Falls back to `username`. **Applies to humans AND bots.** This is the resolution of the earlier "login handle vs alias" open question — yes, we have both, but `display_name` is optional, not enforced.

## Per-bot config in `bots` table

A new `bots` table holds personality + behavior per bot:
- `user_id` (PK, FK → users.id)
- `system_prompt text NOT NULL` — the personality. Edited via SQL or `bot:edit` CLI.
- `auto_join_new_rooms boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT false` — `true` only for the primary helpful assistant (`claude`). Personas (Kobe, Moses, etc.) are `false` and invited per-room.
- `enabled boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT true`

## Mention semantics

- Pattern: `@<username>` anywhere in a message, case-insensitive match against `users.username`
- First bot mention wins (multi-bot fanout deferred)
- Bots can't trigger bots — messages authored by `is_bot=true` users are skipped at the parser
- Bot must be a `roomMembers` row in the target room to be mentionable there

## Dispatch flow

1. Human sends message → route handler inserts + broadcasts + returns 201 immediately
2. Async: parse mentions, find matching bot member
3. Broadcast `{type: "typing", userId, username, roomId}` over WS
4. Build provider payload: last 20 messages with `"username: text"` prefix on user turns, plain content on bot turns, system prompt from `bots.system_prompt`
5. Call provider.chat() — Anthropic for v1, OpenAI-compat available via swap
6. Insert bot's reply as a `messages` row, broadcast as normal message
7. Broadcast `{type: "typing_stop", userId, roomId}`
8. On error: log via pino, post short "I'm having trouble responding…" message, broadcast stop

## Locked decisions

- **History window:** 20 messages of room history fed to the LLM per reply
- **Max reply tokens:** 500 (chat bubbles, not essays)
- **Provider for v1:** Anthropic only
- **Default bot:** `claude`, auto-joins new rooms, helpful/dry/direct system prompt
- **Personas:** added via `npm run bot:create` CLI, invited to rooms via `npm run bot:invite`
- **Typing indicator:** YES — both `typing` and `typing_stop` WS message types
- **Error visibility:** YES — user sees a graceful failure message in chat. Server logs everything via pino regardless.
- **UI scrollback:** unlimited, paginated 50 at a time. Server-side already supports `?before=`. Client-side scroll-to-load is a separate follow-up, NOT part of bot v1.

## Bots and the staking graph

Bots are not stakeable (per `social_graph_staking.md`). The `is_bot` flag is the explicit exclusion. Bots can be members of stake-gated rooms in the future; the gating predicate just doesn't apply to them.

## Why not OpenClaw or similar agent platforms

OpenClaw (openclaw.ai) is a personal AI assistant with shell/file/browser tools. Overkill — and a security risk — for a multi-user chat reply bot. The provider abstraction approach above gives `@<botname>` chat without exposing real-world action capability. Real agent integration (tools, actions) is a Phase 4+ concern, not v1.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: Social graph — staking for humanness via in-person pledge ritual
3	description: How users stake each other (QR + code + selfie ritual) and what that implies for chat/wallet/bot design
4	type: project
5	originSessionId: 30c264ac-2abc-4e83-9020-c83e0856f83f
6	---
7	Scoot is member-only and small at first, may grow. Pseudonymity is allowed — real identities are not required. The platform doesn't need to know who you really are, but it tracks who has vouched that you're a person.
8	
9	## The pledge ritual (how staking actually happens)
10	
11	Staking is an in-person ceremony between an existing user (the *staker*, A) and a prospect (the *stakee*, B). It cannot be performed remotely.
12	
13	1. **A generates a QR code** on their device.
14	2. **B scans A's QR**, opening a pledge-in-progress on B's side.
15	3. **A speaks a one-time code aloud.** B **types it into B's phone**, which transmits the typed code back to A's phone for verification. A confirms the match. This proves real-time co-presence (B heard the spoken code and entered it on B's own device). *Note: this exact flow is provisional — may be revised. The intent is "A's voice + B's input + A's verification" so a screen-scraped QR alone is insufficient.*
16	4. **A second scan** completes the cryptographic handshake (likely A scans a QR B now presents).
17	5. **A takes a selfie with B.** This selfie is saved permanently in A's **"staked pledges"** listing.
18	6. **B is now staked-by-A.** B can subsequently stake others, building out a chain of trust.
19	
20	Acknowledged: the verbal-code step is not perfectly secure if the staker is willing to cheat (e.g. relays the code remotely). The system trusts the staker as the human-in-the-loop verifier — see "Real-world context" below.
21	
22	The result is a **directed graph** (often tree-like, but cycles possible): every staked user can be traced back through one or more stakers to earlier members. The chain is the trust artifact.
23	
24	## Real-world context (why the ritual works this way)
25	
26	Scoot operates out of a city rec center. People drop in, get staked, and may not be seen again for *years* before reappearing. The community is intermittent and partly anonymous.
27	
28	**The selfie exists to solve a specific problem: the staker's own memory.** It is *not* primarily a public proof of pledge. When user X comes back after three years and says "hey, you staked me," the staker needs to be able to look at their staked-pledges list, see X's selfie next to their face today, and confirm "yes, I remember this person, I vouched for them."
29	
30	This shapes several design decisions:
31	- **The selfie's audience is the staker first.** Default visibility should be private-to-staker (and probably the stakee themselves). Wider visibility is a separate, opt-in feature.
32	- **Selfies must be durable.** They need to survive years of storage and remain recognizable.
33	- **The staker is the human-in-the-loop verifier.** For now, "is this a real human" is determined by the staker physically meeting the prospect and forming a personal judgment. The platform does not try to verify humanness independently — it trusts the staker's eyes.
34	- **The verification standard is broad: not AI, not bot, not non-human biological.** The staker is asserting "I was in the same room as this person and they are a human being." That's the bar. No identity verification beyond that.
35	
36	## Design implications
37	
38	- **Trust/visibility decisions** (who can DM whom, who can join rooms, what the bot trusts) should be driven by the staking graph — distance from a known root, depth of stakers, etc. — not by usernames or emails.
39	- **The bot lives in chat alongside humans.** Staking is how a human proves they're not another bot. Bots are not stakeable.
40	- **The Scoot token is the obvious collateral.** Stakers may risk Scoot when they pledge — bad pledges (later revoked) could slash. This ties wallet directly to social graph.
41	- **Selfies are first-class data, and private by default.** Pledge selfies live in the staker's private list — they are not public attestations. Storage, display, and access control must reflect "the staker is the audience" — wider visibility is opt-in only.
42	- **Onboarding requires physical access to an existing member.** No self-serve signup beyond the seeded root user(s). The auth lock-down (registration disabled, default user seeded) already aligns with this.
43	- **Room privacy / DM gating should be expressible in graph terms** ("staked by someone in this room", "≤ 2 hops from member X", "any staked user") — not just static member lists.
44	- **No heavy identity/KYC flows.** Pseudonymity is a feature; the pledge ritual replaces KYC for the "is human" question.
45	
46	## Revocation
47	
48	Revocation rules depend on whether the pledge was a valid humanness assertion or not:
49	
50	- **If the pledge represents a confirmed human** (the staker did meet a real person and validated them): revocation is restricted. Either (a) **admin-only**, or (b) **both the staker AND the staker's own staker must agree**. Open: pick one of these or allow either path. The intent is that you can't casually un-vouch for a confirmed human — that's a serious action that should require either platform-level authority or a small consensus up the chain.
51	- **If the pledge was bogus** — the staker was tricked (the prospect wasn't actually human / wasn't unique), or the staker didn't follow the ritual rules — **revocation is freely allowed.** This path exists to clean up mistakes and rule-breaking without needing consensus.
52	
53	The data model needs to capture *which kind* of revocation a pledge supports at revoke-time (i.e. a "humanness was confirmed" flag, or the ability to reclassify a pledge as bogus before/at revocation).
54	
55	Downstream impact of revocation on the staking graph (do B's own pledges get weakened? cascade-revoked? unaffected?) is an open question — defer until building the staking-gated chat/wallet features.
56	
57	## Root of trust
58	
59	**Single global root** — currently user `scuzzydude` (the developer). May be renamed to `rocketman` later. There is exactly one root staker for the platform; all other members trace their pledge chain back to this root.
60	
61	This aligns with the existing auth lock-down: registration is disabled, the default user is seeded from env vars (`49be5be`), and that seeded user *is* the root.
62	
63	## Handle model (open)
64	
65	Considering a **two-handle system**:
66	- **Login handle** — stable, identity-bearing, used for auth and as the canonical user identifier in the staking graph.
67	- **Alias / message handle (nickname)** — display name shown in chat, mutable, may be changed without affecting identity or pledges.
68	
69	This is not decided yet. If adopted, the schema needs `username` (login) + `display_name` (alias) on the users table, and chat UI should render the alias while staking/admin views render the login handle.
70	
71	## How to apply
72	
73	- When designing chat scope: model permissions as graph queries against the staking graph, not as role tables.
74	- When designing wallet scope: leave room for stake transactions (lock/escrow Scoot against another user) and for slashing on revoked pledges.
75	- When designing room membership: visibility rules should accept graph predicates as first-class inputs.
76	- When building features that involve user-to-user trust, ask "could this be expressed via staking?" before adding admin/moderator flows.
77

---
name: Social graph — staking for humanness via in-person pledge ritual
description: How users stake each other (QR + code + selfie ritual) and what that implies for chat/wallet/bot design
type: project
originSessionId: 30c264ac-2abc-4e83-9020-c83e0856f83f
---
Scoot is member-only and small at first, may grow. Pseudonymity is allowed — real identities are not required. The platform doesn't need to know who you really are, but it tracks who has vouched that you're a person.

## The pledge ritual (how staking actually happens)

Staking is an in-person ceremony between an existing user (the *staker*, A) and a prospect (the *stakee*, B). It cannot be performed remotely.

1. **A generates a QR code** on their device.
2. **B scans A's QR**, opening a pledge-in-progress on B's side.
3. **A speaks a one-time code aloud.** B **types it into B's phone**, which transmits the typed code back to A's phone for verification. A confirms the match. This proves real-time co-presence (B heard the spoken code and entered it on B's own device). *Note: this exact flow is provisional — may be revised. The intent is "A's voice + B's input + A's verification" so a screen-scraped QR alone is insufficient.*
4. **A second scan** completes the cryptographic handshake (likely A scans a QR B now presents).
5. **A takes a selfie with B.** This selfie is saved permanently in A's **"staked pledges"** listing.
6. **B is now staked-by-A.** B can subsequently stake others, building out a chain of trust.

Acknowledged: the verbal-code step is not perfectly secure if the staker is willing to cheat (e.g. relays the code remotely). The system trusts the staker as the human-in-the-loop verifier — see "Real-world context" below.

The result is a **directed graph** (often tree-like, but cycles possible): every staked user can be traced back through one or more stakers to earlier members. The chain is the trust artifact.

## Real-world context (why the ritual works this way)

Scoot operates out of a city rec center. People drop in, get staked, and may not be seen again for *years* before reappearing. The community is intermittent and partly anonymous.

**The selfie exists to solve a specific problem: the staker's own memory.** It is *not* primarily a public proof of pledge. When user X comes back after three years and says "hey, you staked me," the staker needs to be able to look at their staked-pledges list, see X's selfie next to their face today, and confirm "yes, I remember this person, I vouched for them."

This shapes several design decisions:
- **The selfie's audience is the staker first.** Default visibility should be private-to-staker (and probably the stakee themselves). Wider visibility is a separate, opt-in feature.
- **Selfies must be durable.** They need to survive years of storage and remain recognizable.
- **The staker is the human-in-the-loop verifier.** For now, "is this a real human" is determined by the staker physically meeting the prospect and forming a personal judgment. The platform does not try to verify humanness independently — it trusts the staker's eyes.
- **The verification standard is broad: not AI, not bot, not non-human biological.** The staker is asserting "I was in the same room as this person and they are a human being." That's the bar. No identity verification beyond that.

## Design implications

- **Trust/visibility decisions** (who can DM whom, who can join rooms, what the bot trusts) should be driven by the staking graph — distance from a known root, depth of stakers, etc. — not by usernames or emails.
- **The bot lives in chat alongside humans.** Staking is how a human proves they're not another bot. Bots are not stakeable.
- **The Scoot token is the obvious collateral.** Stakers may risk Scoot when they pledge — bad pledges (later revoked) could slash. This ties wallet directly to social graph.
- **Selfies are first-class data, and private by default.** Pledge selfies live in the staker's private list — they are not public attestations. Storage, display, and access control must reflect "the staker is the audience" — wider visibility is opt-in only.
- **Onboarding requires physical access to an existing member.** No self-serve signup beyond the seeded root user(s). The auth lock-down (registration disabled, default user seeded) already aligns with this.
- **Room privacy / DM gating should be expressible in graph terms** ("staked by someone in this room", "≤ 2 hops from member X", "any staked user") — not just static member lists.
- **No heavy identity/KYC flows.** Pseudonymity is a feature; the pledge ritual replaces KYC for the "is human" question.

## Revocation

Revocation rules depend on whether the pledge was a valid humanness assertion or not:

- **If the pledge represents a confirmed human** (the staker did meet a real person and validated them): revocation is restricted. Either (a) **admin-only**, or (b) **both the staker AND the staker's own staker must agree**. Open: pick one of these or allow either path. The intent is that you can't casually un-vouch for a confirmed human — that's a serious action that should require either platform-level authority or a small consensus up the chain.
- **If the pledge was bogus** — the staker was tricked (the prospect wasn't actually human / wasn't unique), or the staker didn't follow the ritual rules — **revocation is freely allowed.** This path exists to clean up mistakes and rule-breaking without needing consensus.

The data model needs to capture *which kind* of revocation a pledge supports at revoke-time (i.e. a "humanness was confirmed" flag, or the ability to reclassify a pledge as bogus before/at revocation).

Downstream impact of revocation on the staking graph (do B's own pledges get weakened? cascade-revoked? unaffected?) is an open question — defer until building the staking-gated chat/wallet features.

## Root of trust

**Single global root** — currently user `scuzzydude` (the developer). May be renamed to `rocketman` later. There is exactly one root staker for the platform; all other members trace their pledge chain back to this root.

This aligns with the existing auth lock-down: registration is disabled, the default user is seeded from env vars (`49be5be`), and that seeded user *is* the root.

## Handle model (open)

Considering a **two-handle system**:
- **Login handle** — stable, identity-bearing, used for auth and as the canonical user identifier in the staking graph.
- **Alias / message handle (nickname)** — display name shown in chat, mutable, may be changed without affecting identity or pledges.

This is not decided yet. If adopted, the schema needs `username` (login) + `display_name` (alias) on the users table, and chat UI should render the alias while staking/admin views render the login handle.

## How to apply

- When designing chat scope: model permissions as graph queries against the staking graph, not as role tables.
- When designing wallet scope: leave room for stake transactions (lock/escrow Scoot against another user) and for slashing on revoked pledges.
- When designing room membership: visibility rules should accept graph predicates as first-class inputs.
- When building features that involve user-to-user trust, ask "could this be expressed via staking?" before adding admin/moderator flows.

content.text:
## Where we left off

Last commit (29d73c1) finished bot v1 — the chat bot feature is fully shipped:
- `@<botname>` mentions trigger async LLM replies, typing indicators, bot UI badges
- `claude` bot seeded; `bot:create` / `bot:edit` / `bot:invite` CLIs work
- 25 self-tests passing (parser + dispatcher)
- Seed logic now correctly distinguishes bot users from human users

## Next step per the plan

Per `project_plan.md`, we're at **priority 2: chat polish** — three additive items, no identity dependency:

1. **Read receipts + unread counts** — per-room last-seen, badge in room list
2. **DMs** — 1:1 rooms (likely a `is_dm` flag on rooms + a creation flow)
3. **Image attachments** — upload, storage, render in message bubbles

Then **priority 3: staking ritual** (the QR + code + selfie ceremony) — that's the big identity primitive, blocks everything trust-gated.

## On the architecture conversation

Happy to dig in. A few angles worth picking from — which interests you most?

- **Data flow & ownership boundaries** — Postgres-first today, scootchain later; how/where the cutover happens, what stays in Postgres permanently vs. migrates to the chain
- **The staking graph as a first-class system** — schema, query patterns, how room/DM/wallet permissions become graph predicates instead of role tables
- **Token ↔ staking coupling** — escrow, slashing on revocation, how the wallet API surface looks before scootchain exists
- **Bot/agent expansion** — multi-bot fanout, tool use, the OpenClaw-vs-chat-replies line you already drew
- **Media architecture** — image attachments now, HLS video + share protection later, signed URLs and access control (especially for pledge selfies which are private-to-staker)

What do you want to start with — pick the next code task, or talk arch first?

content: Bot v1 is shipped and committed; next up is chat polish (read receipts + unread counts, DMs, image attachments) before moving on to the staking ritual. Pick one of those three to start, or talk architecture first. (disable recaps in /config)

Found it: `/mnt/c/source/asimov_v2.13.pdf` — 2.9 MB, **335 pages**, PDF v1.7. Here's what I'm planning to do, in order:

## Restated plan

1. **Copy the PDF into the repo** at `docs/reference/asimov_v2.13.pdf` (new directory). Note: this is a binary, ~3 MB — git handles it but it's permanent in history.
2. **Commit + push** the PDF on its own (`docs: add asimov_v2.13.pdf reference`).
3. **Convert PDF → Markdown** at `docs/reference/asimov_v2.13.md`. For 335 pages, plan is `pdftotext -layout` → light cleanup (heading detection, blank-line collapsing) into Markdown. This is *text extraction* quality, not OCR or layout-preserving — tables/figures will be lossy. If you want higher fidelity, say so and I'll use a heavier tool (e.g. `marker`, `pdf2md`, or page-by-page LLM).
4. **Commit + push** the Markdown separately (`docs: convert asimov_v2.13 to markdown`).
5. **Create a reference-type memory entry** at `~/.claude/projects/.../memory/asimov_v2_13.md` that points at the in-repo Markdown — so future conversations know the book exists, why it's in the repo (Scoot(34) club reference), and where to read it. The book content itself stays in the repo, not in the memory dir.

content.text:
There are two distinct layers, and your question conflates them — let me untangle:

## Layer 1: Always-loaded (small, every conversation)

Things I see every turn without being told:
- **`CLAUDE.md`** (repo root) — loaded at session start. ~10 KB. Project rules.
- **`MEMORY.md`** (in `~/.claude/.../memory/`) — loaded every turn. An *index* of one-liners.

These have to stay small. A 335-page book absolutely cannot live here — it would blow the context window every turn.

## Layer 2: On-demand (large, read when relevant)

Everything else in the repo, and individual memory files. I only open them when something points me there.

A book belongs in Layer 2. The trick is leaving a **pointer in Layer 1** so I know to go open it.

## So the answer

**Book content (PDF + MD):** lives in the repo at a path like `docs/reference/asimov_v2.13.{pdf,md}`. No `docs/` exists yet — I'd create it. Sibling to `SPEC.md` / `SETUP.md` but nested so we have a clean home for future reference material.

**Pointers (so I actually notice it):** one or both of:
- A line in **`CLAUDE.md`** under a new "Reference material" section: `docs/reference/asimov_v2.13.md — Scoot(34) / Asimov reference. Read when working on Scoot(34) features.`
- A **reference-type memory entry** with the same pointer.

The CLAUDE.md line is project-scoped (lives with the repo, every machine sees it). The memory entry is user-scoped (follows you, not the repo). For a book that's part of *this* project, **CLAUDE.md is the better home** — I'd add it there and skip the memory entry, or add both.

## My recommendation

```
docs/reference/asimov_v2.13.pdf    ← binary, checked in
docs/reference/asimov_v2.13.md     ← converted text
CLAUDE.md                          ← add one paragraph pointing at the .md
```

Want me to go with that, or do you have a different structure in mind? And the other two open questions still stand — conversion fidelity (fast `pdftotext` vs. higher-quality tool), and whether the bot will eventually ingest this for RAG.

> Converted from `asimov_v2.13.pdf` via pdf-parse on 2026-05-13.

asimov
An Equitable Intrinsic Value System
or
a post-harden rockets cathartic
SECOND FOUNDATION
Version 2.13

-- 1 of 335 --

2023 by Brandon Awbrey. This publication may be
reproduced in whole form if fairly attributed for any non-commercial
purpose. If you are using this for commercial purposes, you better
make sure that Brandon is getting paid, unless you are Trey Parker
and Matt Stone, then you guys can make bank. If you paid me for a
printed or eBook form of this book, then thank you. If you paid
somebody else, I join you in a curse.
Cover Art and Illustrations ©2023 by Jack Awbrey
Lyrics are reprinted by permission, copyrights listed in
Acknowledgements.

-- 2 of 335 --

1
Intrinsic Value
There are only two true hedges against inflation. History and
archaeology have proven they’ve both existed for as long as
humanity.
It’s Not real estate. That’s a good one, as Mark Twain said, “Buy
land, they’re not making it anymore.” Elon Musk is trying to change
that maxim. If Mr. Clemens were alive today, he’d have already
bought his tickets to Mars. It’s not true even on Earth. Tax
foreclosures, floods, Texas ice storms, hurricanes, earthquakes, mud
slides, and rising sea levels must be considered, plus don’t forget war
and revolution can put bustles in your hedgerow.
It’s not gold. We tried that. Think gold is your savior as the world
collapses? Good luck. Try pitching a gold bar at a zombie, see how
that works out.
In the old days, if you had gold or land, it was most likely because
you were born to it, or you took it. Wealth was bestowed upon you
by your creator, either by giving you wealthy parents or by blessing
you with the skills and fortune to win at battle.
In modern times, we feel that wealth is a result of humanity's
creations. Through both the industrial and information revolutions,
it has been man’s ability to innovate that has led directly to wealth
and prosperity.
It follows that humanity should use its own creation as the
fundamental measure of value. Floating fiat currencies like the dollar
and cryptocurrencies like bitcoin meet this criteria. Neither dollar nor
bitcoin represent any intrinsic value, and thus can’t really hold value
over generations and through calamity without intervention. Fiat
and cryptocurrencies function as money – a medium of exchange –
which is a technical function of a currency. Neither represent real

content.content:
560	nation of divided values, and every country has a distribution of
561	values among individuals. The system I propose allows those with
562	shared values, wherever they are, to unite and form a common
563	currency of shared responsibility for those ideas. If those who unite
564	aren’t very responsible, then the value relative to other ideas will
565	decline. That’s the way it should be. Good ideas appreciate, vain
566	ideas cycle in value with fashion.
567	The Foundation is a system of competitive self-governance; where
568	the best ideas, managed by the best teams of people, compete for
569	the relative valuation of ideas they support. The cumulative valuation
570	of ideas is measured using a digital currency called asimov. The
571	Foundation’s mission is to commit to ideas of genius, and neglect
572	ideas of vanity. Only history will be able to judge which is which, but
573	over time, the Foundation should accumulate data that should help
574	guide us. The self-governing units are called Scoot, and they issue
575	their own tokens of responsibility also called scoot (lowercase). Scoot
576	can trade for each other and for asimov but are in a restricted system
577	called the responsibility domain. Asimov may trade openly like
578	bitcoin. Through this system, we have a valuation of genius and
579	vanity. The sum of the blockchains becomes a tree of human
580	knowledge, distributed, and replicated throughout the planet by the
581	nodes that process transactions.
582	The greatest treasure may be the future knowledge of how our
583	values change over time, and what values we should instill in our
584	children so that may live in a peaceful, sustainable, and equitable
585	world.
586	Scoot - Dynamically Scalable Responsibility
587	Why Scoot? Scoot is money, y’all.
588	The power of this system is that it uses the innovation of
589	cryptocurrency to provide a scalable system of limited direct
590	governance as well as an immutable historical record of that
591	governance. The protocol will allow you to customize the
592	elective governance of a Scoot. If you want a proportional
593	
594	-- 17 of 335 --
595	
596	16
597	democracy, like a shareholder in a company, you can implement that.
598	Want a representative democracy, because things work so well in
599	your local congressional district? Give that a try. If you want a one-
600	vote direct democracy, because you like participating in social media
601	flame wars, go for it. If you’d like more of a Game of Thrones strategic
602	power moves of royalty without all the blood, you can do that,
603	too. You want to be like Linus, get your dictatorship on. I don’t know
604	which one will be better. Data we gather over years, decades, and
605	centuries will show us which one of these works best or may guide
606	us towards new forms of self-governance that are more efficient.
607	There is a reason direct democracy can’t work on a large scale. We
608	elect people at the local, state, and federal level (who are good at
609	one thing) to lead and manage our governments, but then we expect
610	them to be good at many things. What does a lawyer know about
611	running a water or sewer system, or green recycling, or road
612	maintenance? Elected leaders can hire unbiased, unconflicted
613	experts to do the work, but how proficient can somebody become at
614	a job specific to a region in the short duration of an election cycle?6
615	We should have institutional experience in our government
616	departments that are competent, but how do any of those people
617	take risks or point out problems when their bosses change hands at
618	the whim of the elected leaders?

nation of divided values, and every country has a distribution of
values among individuals. The system I propose allows those with
shared values, wherever they are, to unite and form a common
currency of shared responsibility for those ideas. If those who unite
aren’t very responsible, then the value relative to other ideas will
decline. That’s the way it should be. Good ideas appreciate, vain
ideas cycle in value with fashion.
The Foundation is a system of competitive self-governance; where
the best ideas, managed by the best teams of people, compete for
the relative valuation of ideas they support. The cumulative valuation
of ideas is measured using a digital currency called asimov. The
Foundation’s mission is to commit to ideas of genius, and neglect
ideas of vanity. Only history will be able to judge which is which, but
over time, the Foundation should accumulate data that should help
guide us. The self-governing units are called Scoot, and they issue
their own tokens of responsibility also called scoot (lowercase). Scoot
can trade for each other and for asimov but are in a restricted system
called the responsibility domain. Asimov may trade openly like
bitcoin. Through this system, we have a valuation of genius and
vanity. The sum of the blockchains becomes a tree of human
knowledge, distributed, and replicated throughout the planet by the
nodes that process transactions.
The greatest treasure may be the future knowledge of how our
values change over time, and what values we should instill in our
children so that may live in a peaceful, sustainable, and equitable
world.
Scoot - Dynamically Scalable Responsibility
Why Scoot? Scoot is money, y’all.
The power of this system is that it uses the innovation of
cryptocurrency to provide a scalable system of limited direct
governance as well as an immutable historical record of that
governance. The protocol will allow you to customize the
elective governance of a Scoot. If you want a proportional

-- 17 of 335 --

16
democracy, like a shareholder in a company, you can implement that.
Want a representative democracy, because things work so well in
your local congressional district? Give that a try. If you want a one-
vote direct democracy, because you like participating in social media
flame wars, go for it. If you’d like more of a Game of Thrones strategic
power moves of royalty without all the blood, you can do that,
too. You want to be like Linus, get your dictatorship on. I don’t know
which one will be better. Data we gather over years, decades, and
centuries will show us which one of these works best or may guide
us towards new forms of self-governance that are more efficient.
There is a reason direct democracy can’t work on a large scale. We
elect people at the local, state, and federal level (who are good at
one thing) to lead and manage our governments, but then we expect
them to be good at many things. What does a lawyer know about
running a water or sewer system, or green recycling, or road
maintenance? Elected leaders can hire unbiased, unconflicted
experts to do the work, but how proficient can somebody become at
a job specific to a region in the short duration of an election cycle?6
We should have institutional experience in our government
departments that are competent, but how do any of those people
take risks or point out problems when their bosses change hands at
the whim of the elected leaders?
The intact bureaucracy makes this whole process complicated
because the scope is too broad. A congressman can get stuff done in
government – he just makes a phone call and the water pumping
station in his district gets a new pump ahead of some other
district. All it cost was a vote against his conscience and the
consensus of his constituents on a voting rights bill. People who can
already vote are likely to remember the high-pressure shower they
get each morning when election time rolls around again.
Limited scope governance can be efficient. Take a Homeowners
Association (HOA). You have an hour meeting once a
month. Hopefully, the chairman of the HOA is somebody practical
6 And what kind of unbiased, unconflicted real expert wants to work for a politician?

The Foundation is the organization that governs the system. The

Pledges are members of The Foundation. Pledges are identified by

Deterministic biometric identity. The Foundation uses biometric

The scootage are the holders of a specific scoot.

A person elected or chosen by the scootage as leader. The

8575	for a legacy of genius. Each Scoot shall have a charter, clearly stating
8576	the purpose and method of governance, which shall be enforceable
8577	via methods in the protocol. Holders of scoot have no rights other
8578	than the elective governance of the Scoot, but a responsibility – a
8579	pledge – to increase the appreciation of the subject of the Scoot.

8616	are processed through a network of registered nodes and
8617	anonymous nodes.
8618	Pledges are people registered with The Foundation. Pledges may
8619	not be organizations or legal entities. Trade in scoot occurs in the
8620	responsibility domain. The responsibility domain trades only in scoot,
8621	and no direct trade with fiat or crypto. Only registered nodes can
8622	operate in the responsibility domain, and registered nodes can only
8623	be individual pledges or scoot trustees. Only The Foundation,
8624	pledges, or other Scoot can be holders of scoot.
8625	Once legally formed, a Scoot elects a trustee who proxies the
8626	Scoot’s own trading (genesis) address. A Scoot may generate income,
8627	but that income can only be spent on the appreciation of the Scoot.
8628	Funds may be used for some direct appreciation effort, or to
8629	purchase the scoot in the responsibility domain to decrease liquidity.
8630	The Foundation will issue genesis asimov in two cases. Initially
8631	during the staking, seeding, and development stages as described in
8632	the Circulation chapter. Moving forward, The Foundation will only
8633	issue genesis asimov in exchange for scoot. A total of one trillion
8634	asimov will be minted and disbursed over 200 years according to the
8635	distribution schedule outlined in Appendix A.
8636	After a Scoot is legally formed, it may participate in responsibility
8637	domain by registering the Scoot’s address. The Foundation shall
8638	charge a fee, in asimov, for this registration. The fee will be high
8639	enough to discourage novelty scooting. There shall be a staking
8640	program where pledges can stake new Scoot and reduce the cost of
8641	registration. Credits towards registration will be earned through
8642	
8643	-- 244 of 335 --
8644	
8645	243
8646	transaction processing in the responsibility domain by pledges and
8647	nodes.
8648	At Scoot registration, the trustee may trade The Foundation for
8649	the new genesis scoot at a negotiated rate in asimov. This schedule
8650	of commitment offered by The Foundation shall be published and
8651	unbiased based on content. The Foundation may decline to make an
8652	offer arbitrarily. This would typically be to avoid participation in
8653	novelty scoot or obvious vanity. A Scoot does not need to be held by
8654	The Foundation for it to participate in the network.
8655	By collecting newly minted scoot, asimov is an index that reflects
8656	the value of all Scoot.
8657	Holders of a particular scoot are known collectively as the
8658	scootage.
8659	The end goal is for the Foundation to hold all scoot of genius and
8660	none of vanity. This is an impossible task. Genius of today may be
8661	vanity of the future. In macro-economic terms, it gives us an
8662	equivalency. When The Foundation is “caught up,” all of the asimov
8663	will be circulating, and the Foundation should hold in reserve only
8664	scoot of “genius.”
8665	Property of Wealth (POW)
8666	One of my high-minded goals for this project is to describe the
8667	human system of value that is understandable using terminology and
8668	examples that are relatable to everyday life. A correlated goal was
8669	that I do not spend any of my own personal property wealth feeding
8670	lawyers.
8671	Property of Wealth is my term. I did some research looking for the
8672	correct legal or accounting terminology for what I wanted to convey.
8673	I quickly realized that while it’s easy to explain and understand, it’s
8674	difficult to specify in a deterministic manner. This may be one reason
8675	the U.S. Tax code is 70,000 pages long.134
8676	134 How Many Words Are in the Tax Code: https://taxfoundation.org/how-many-
8677	words-are-tax-code/. Another factor is that lawyers get paid by the hour and write
8678	really slowly.
8679	
8680	-- 245 of 335 --
8681	
8682	244
8683	Da Vinci’s Mona Lisa is a Property of Wealth. A Da Vinci coloring
8684	book handed out to kids who visit the Louvre is not.
8685	The New York Yankees baseball team is a Property of Wealth. A
8686	Yankee ball cap is not.
8687	A first edition printing of Mark Twain’s Adventures of Huckleberry
8688	Finn is a Property of Wealth. A paperback copy you picked up at the
8689	thrift shop is not.
8690	Ultimately, determining what is Property of Wealth and what is
8691	just property is the responsibility of the trustee and the scootage.
8692	Keeping “property” out of the responsibility domain is the
8693	responsibility of that domain. It is a good term for that reason.
8694	Scoot Conformance
8695	1. A Scoot is not property. It is a shared responsibility for an
8696	idea.
8697	2. A Scoot shall have a written charter stating:
8698	a. Subject of the appreciation of the Scoot.
8699	b. Method of governance.
8700	i. Governance must conform to a method
8701	executable by the protocol.
8702	c. Maximum reserve holdings of cash or other forms of
8703	currency or material inventory.
8704	i. Scoot may hold unlimited asimov or any other
8705	scoot.
8706	d. Default burn method for excess holdings.
8707	e. The written charter shall be the an entry on the
8708	scootchain.
8709	3. A Scoot may not own real property of any kind.
8710	4. A Scoot may purchase Properties of Wealth (POW)
8711	a. A Scoot may buy non-real Properties of Wealth
8712	(anything but land/real estate).
8713	b. Property of wealth bought by a Scoot must be
8714	immediately gifted to humanity at large.
8715	i. In jurisdictions where gifting title to humanity
8716	at large does not have legal significance,
8717	retaining formal title is acceptable as long a
8718	
8719	-- 246 of 335 --
8720	
8721	245
8722	written and ratified statement of the gift is
8723	published on both scootchain and local
8724	publication of record.
8725	ii. Property bought and gifted by the Scoot is
8726	considered a global public good by The
8727	Foundation and its pledges.
8728	c. If legally feasible, Scoot may retain right of possession
8729	of that property.
8730	i. Possession alone should be adequate, as it
8731	usually implies ownership where no clear legal
8732	title can be established.
8733	ii. Formal custodianship and tax-liability should
8734	be established where legally feasible.
8735	d. The Scoot shall be responsible for all taxes and legal
8736	encumberments of any property bought.
8737	e. A Scoot may never sell property of wealth.
8738	f. Scoot may never use property as collateral.
8739	5. A Scoot may act as a conduit of income for the subject of the
8740	Scoot
8741	a. Conduit income must be recorded on the scootchain.
8742	6. A Scoot may earn income.
8743	a. A Scoot is responsible for any taxes or
8744	encumberments on that income.
8745	b. That income may be commitment fees from the
8746	scootage
8747	c. Income may only be spent to further the appreciation
8748	of the subject of the Scoot.
8749	i. Educational efforts.
8750	ii. Derivative efforts.
8751	iii. Inspirational efforts.
8752	iv. Acquiring related or competitive scoot.
8753	v. Buybacks of the subject scoot to decrease
8754	liquidity.
8755	d. A Scoot may not use income or any other inducement
8756	to influence governments, directly or indirectly.
8757	
8758	-- 247 of 335 --
8759	
8760	246
8761	e. Income or material benefit may never be transferred
8762	to the scootage, directly or indirectly.
8763	f. Scoot may buy raw goods to produce and sell material
8764	goods or services if it:
8765	i. Operates as the responsible party for a non-
8766	profit legal entity formed under the local
8767	jurisdiction of the Scoot’s registration
8768	location.
8769	ii. Prices must be proportional to cost and max
8770	extent is published in the charter and Scoot
8771	Protocol Genesis Block.
8772	iii. Publishes tax filings on the scootchain.
8773	iv. Specifies limits of markup and salaries of
8774	employees in the charter.
8775	Scootchain
8776	The scootchain is the transactional blockchain for each Scoot. It
8777	also serves as an immutable record of digital content as well as a legal
8778	record of any physical property transferred to the public domain
8779	through action of the Scoot.
8780	The goal is that each scootchain is secured and geographically
8781	distributed to preserve value through time and calamity. The Scoot
8782	node shall maintain the master copy of the scootchain, and when The
8783	Foundation is a holder, it will also duplicate the scootchain. In
8784	addition, Scoot may form staking groups with other Scoot with
8785	agreements to duplicate each other’s scootchains and process
8786	transactions. Pledge nodes (could be a phone or other client device)
8787	can participate in staking and earn credits.
8788	Responsibility Tree
8789	Participants in the responsibility domain are known to The
8790	Foundation by their human identities. The Foundation will
8791	implement a secure validation process that will require some form of
8792	deterministic biometric identification. If you break the rules and The
8793	Foundation kicks you off the responsibility domain, plastic surgery
8794	and a new passport isn’t going to help you.
8795	
8796	-- 248 of 335 --
8797	
8798	247
8799	The Address scheme shall allow for single pledge wallet (private
8800	key) to have multiple valid addresses. Addresses that resolve to the
8801	pledges identity via API are True Pledge Handles (TPH). Addresses
8802	that do not resolve to TPH are pseudonymous pledge handles (PPH).
8803	TPH will resolve to the equivalent of an ID Number and the
8804	Country of registration. Additional details require the pledge to
8805	explicitly share the information. Some people will wish to share their
8806	real-world identities, and some will not.
8807	How this is managed both globally and on a per-Scoot basis is
8808	prime topic for debate. This is people’s wealth, and there is an
8809	expectation of privacy for both security and competitive reasons.
8810	There is also the traditional personal feeling that one’s money is
8811	one’s own business. Scoot wealth is not assets, but a person’s
8812	commitment of responsibility, but the same privacy requirements
8813	will apply.
8814	The system derives its value from the responsibility of individuals.
8815	Some people are more responsible than others. Who holds a scoot
8816	can confer a higher value. When a genius artist commits to another
8817	artist’s Scoot, it would add value correlated to the value of the genius
8818	doing the committing. The relationship between scoot and pledges
8819	is the responsibility tree.
8820	PPH would lend itself to always using a new address for each
8821	transaction in the responsibility domain. This would be the strictest
8822	and most limited usage of the responsibility domain. This gives The
8823	Foundation an unfair advantage in evaluating genius, as it has access
8824	to the full responsibility tree with coherent human identities.
8825	Privileged employees of The Foundation would always have full
8826	visibility to what individuals were committing to. This is unavoidable.
8827	To valuate responsibility, you must have accountability.
8828	There will be cases where Scoot wish only to accept transactions
8829	with TPH. It’s possible to have cases where PPH are used, but the TPH
8830	of the PPH is validated to the Scoot trustee. That would require a
8831	level of trust maintenance across elections of trustees and may be
8832	difficult to enforce.
8833	I believe using TPH to invest in some scoot, and PPHs for others
8834	may be the most practical system. The protocol shall handle both
8835	
8836	-- 249 of 335 --
8837	
8838	248
8839	cases. Scoot may also implement hybrid models, where large or
8840	majority holders must reveal TPH but small-scale holders may use
8841	PPH. This is essentially how public stock works in some countries,
8842	when holdings by large institutions, board members, and company
8843	officers are publicly reported, but most holders are publicly
8844	anonymous.
8845	The responsibility tree - open or pseudonymous - is very valuable.
8846	This system works because the more people who are responsible for
8847	an idea, the more valuable that idea becomes. Furthermore, the
8848	more responsible people who are responsible for an idea, the more
8849	valuable it becomes.
8850	The inverse of the responsibility tree is the hypocrisy tree. Within
8851	the protocol, there is a method of shunning. Shunning is a way for
8852	Scoot and pledges to avoid transactions with other Scoot or pledges
8853	it has made a specific value judgement on. When you join a shun, you
8854	are making a statement: I refuse to interact with this Scoot because
8855	it is specifically bad in the way the shun describes. The official
8856	reference version of the protocol software will not accept
8857	transaction from a Scoot you have shunned. This is a choice of
8858	implementation. Nodes and pledges can earn discounts by
8859	processing transactions. The hypocrisy tree would reveal when
8860	nodes transact with nodes they’ve shunned, which will devalue the
8861	importance of a shun. If you joined a shun against one of your
8862	competitors and some calamity is forcing you to liquidate
8863	unexpectedly, the only buyer might be somebody you really don’t
8864	like. As an individual, I don’t think this is a big worry. People’s value
8865	judgements change over time. It should come into consideration
8866	when electing Foundation leadership and Scoot trustees.
8867	Scoot Naming System (SNS)
8868	The Scoot protocol shall only use Scoot indexes in transactions.
8869	In the future, there will be millions or billions of individual Scoot.
8870	The Foundation shall maintain a Scoot Naming System (SNS), like
8871	the Domain Name System of the Internet. Scoot protocol has a field
8872	for unit of scoot, and a sub-unit field. In the protocol, the fields are
8873	
8874	-- 250 of 335 --
8875	
8876	249
8877	not named, and the sub-unit field may either be used as a fractional
8878	component, or a serialization.
8879	The unit and sub-unit fields may be used as a colloquial name for
8880	the Scoot. The name for Scoot(0) is asimov for the unit and the isaac
8881	for the fractional component.
8882	The SNS is a distributed API provided by The Foundation that
8883	provides a text-based lookup of Scoot. Searching on “Asimov” will
8884	always yield scoot(0) as the first entry. There could be dozens or
8885	thousands of other entries, listed by market value in asimov. The
8886	naming convention for human subject based Scoot shall be
8887	scoot(FirstName.LastName) or (SubUnit.Unit). Formally, asimov
8888	should be Scoot(Isaac.Asimov) but since it’s a special case, “asimov”
8889	alone will always refer to scoot(0).
8890	During the initial rollout of the protocol, The Foundation may sell
8891	short-term leases of SNS entries for terms of a year or longer where
8892	there is value-based justification. If the Louvre was to scoot Da Vinci’s
8893	Mona Lisa, then perhaps a 10 or 100 year lease of scoot(Mona.Lisa)
8894	would be warranted. If you’re just the first person to
8895	scoot(William.Shakespeare) there is no explicit value you can assert
8896	just because you were the first – anybody and likely everybody will
8897	think they’ve got the best take on appreciating Shakespeare.
8898	Normal operation of the SNS will make naming assignment
8899	dynamic, based on overall value. Say you started scoot(75161) and
8900	were the first to lease SNS rights to (William.Shakespeare) for one
8901	year. When the year is up, the system will look at the trade-based
8902	valuation of scoot(75161) and count the number of months that
8903	scoot(75161) was the most valuable Scoot with the subject of
8904	William Shakespeare. Let’s say for 7 consecutive months of the last
8905	year it was the most valuable Shakespeare Scoot and ended the year
8906	at the top. That would mean that another Scoot must become the
8907	most valuable Scoot for at least 8 months to take over SNS entry of
8908	(William.Shakespeare). This limit shall have some maximum extent,
8909	a year or two, as determined by The Foundation’s board.
8910	The Foundation can only calculate SNS valuation by trades. One
8911	strategy Scoot trustees can use is once they hold a SNS lease, is to
8912	convince their scootage to hold and not trade once a high-water
8913	
8914	-- 251 of 335 --
8915	
8916	250
8917	mark is established and managing strategic alliances with other Scoot
8918	to up-trade genesis scoot when a competitive threat reveals itself.
8919	Scoot that lose their lease shall default to a list of alternative names
8920	of their choosing.
8921	Holding SNS rights can have very little actual meaning, as it will be
8922	clear in Foundation training of pledges that the SNS rights are
8923	temporary. In API terms, all the SNS entry does is return the scoot
8924	index first in a search.
8925	Scoot Entity
8926	The Foundation should seek to establish Scoot as a legal entity of
8927	Scoot. It may take some time for legal systems to catch up to
8928	understanding what this requires.
8929	Scoot may be implemented as a trust. The beneficiary of the trust
8930	is the public domain. The trustee is elected by the holders of
8931	proportional responsibility as specified by the scootchain, known
8932	collectively as the scootage.
8933	It would be preferable to require all Scoot be formed as a trust or
8934	similar legal entity. The cost of establishing a trust in the U.S. is about
8935	$2000 and that expense could be a way to prevent novelty scooting.
8936	One of the goals of The Foundation is global wealth equalization, and
8937	for that to work, we need for people in every country to be able to
8938	form Scoot without hardship. We must allow individuals to manage
8939	Scoot directly, like a sole proprietorship, without the formality of a
8940	legal entity.
8941	For compliance and conformity, The Foundation should support
8942	efforts to establish “Scoot” via legislation as a low-cost legal entity in
8943	all countries where The Foundation is welcome. This should be a
8944	long-term effort and is not needed to launch the protocol. In this
8945	effort, the Foundation should provide written legal justifications, and
8946	expert testimony, but no funding. The Foundation may not use
8947	asimov, held scoot, cash or any form of property or service to
8948	influence local political leaders, office seekers or government
8949	officials.
8950	
8951	-- 252 of 335 --
8952	
8953	251
8954	This really is a matter of semantics and accounting. The pledges of
8955	a Scoot are fully responsible for the legacy, but they have no
8956	obligation. There is no investment, no dividends, and every
8957	transaction is recorded on a publicly available blockchain. Scoot is an
8958	organization like a fan club, or historical preservation society, or a
8959	reading circle, which uses the Scoot Protocol to manage proportional
8960	responsibility and to elect trustees and vote on propositions. It has
8961	no rights, no exclusivity, and must pay taxes and obey local laws.
8962	The Foundation should establish training and guidelines for
8963	establishing Scoot legally in all countries where it operates.
8964	Scoot may not own property of wealth. Scoot may act as a
8965	custodian for property released to the public domain. Scoot may hold
8966	scoot, including asimov, and may hold income in fiat or other
8967	financial instruments in amounts limited in the scoot charter.
8968	Scoot may generate income from its activities, but that income
8969	may only be used in efforts to increase the appreciation of the
8970	subject genius. This may include competitive mergers and acquisition
8971	of other Scoot, creating a Scoot that is a collection of similar or
8972	divergent work, marketing and educational efforts, and further
8973	derivative work based on the source genius.
8974	This includes buybacks of scoot to decrease holder liquidity and
8975	increase overall market value. This may not work the way it does with
8976	stocks. My contention is that an idea becomes valuable proportional
8977	to the number of individuals responsible for it. Decreasing liquidity
8978	may have the opposite effect and decrease value. The change in
8979	valuation as liquidity changes may be a way to differentiate vanity
8980	from genius over time.
8981	There is no means for a Scoot to distribute income to the
8982	scootage. This is essential to the working of the system, and violators
8983	will be permanently banned from participation in the responsibility
8984	domain.
8985	Becoming wealthy in Scoot means you are responsible for creating
8986	new ideas, appreciating existing ideas, or both.
8987	I’ve given names to different types of Scoot. Their names are
8988	derived from how they deal with existing property and rights. Except
8989	for the conformance guidelines I’ve laid out, there are no rules about
8990	
8991	-- 253 of 335 --
8992	
8993	252
8994	what a Scoot can or should do. Scoot is genius wealth, implement
8995	them the way you want.
8996	
8997	-- 254 of 335 --
8998	
8999	253
9000	Brandon’s First Letter to his Crypto-Advisor
9001	11/17/2023
9002	Dear Captain WXX.I.AX CXXXXX BXXXX,
9003	Scoot is not crypto. Scoot is tradable responsibility.
9004	Technically, Scoot is a voting system. In the responsibility domain,
9005	holding scoot infers no property value, it is tradable responsibility for
9006	the appreciation of ideas. Using crypto technology for electoral
9007	systems is not a new idea, I make no claim to innovation on this front.
9008	The technology I propose as Scoot2 is fundamental blockchain
9009	technology. I am not an expert in crypto, because I don’t care to be,
9010	nor do I wish to intrude upon your realm of expertise. Scoot is simply
9011	using a blockchain to store data and hold elections. I do thank you,
9012	and the changes in version 2.06+ should reflect your input.
9013	According to Wikipedia (using our only common reference, since
9014	I don’t use YouTube), cryptocurrency is a system that must meet six
9015	conditions:
9016	(1)The system does not require a central authority; its state is
9017	maintained through distributed consensus.
9018	No. The Foundation is a central authority, governed by openly
9019	elected humans. The system is designed so that even if asimov
9020	becomes a global currency, individuals, not corporations,
9021	governments, or Artificial Intelligence, control it.
9022	If you’re interested in learning more about what happens when
9023	an AI controls critical human systems, I would refer to the works of
9024	Isaac Asimov. If that’s too much reading, try Kubrick or Cameron.
9025	This is the part crypto people won’t like, and the primary reason
9026	Scoot isn’t crypto. AIs could control “true” cryptocurrencies. So could
9027	
9028	-- 255 of 335 --
9029	
9030	254
9031	public corporations.135 So can douchebags, if you want to go back in
9032	ancient history, like 2022.136
9033	(2) The system keeps an overview of cryptocurrency units and their
9034	ownership.
9035	Yes. I would change the wording, as it applies it to Scoot, to this:
9036	The system keeps an overview of responsibility units and their
9037	pledges.
9038	(3) The system defines whether new cryptocurrency units can be
9039	created. If new cryptocurrency units can be created, the system
9040	defines the circumstances of their origin and how to determine the
9041	ownership of these new units.
9042	Yes. No disagreement in meaning, I would just not apply the
9043	ownership language.
9044	(4) Ownership of cryptocurrency units can be proved exclusively
9045	cryptographically.
9046	No. The units in responsibility domain can be proven exclusively
9047	by validation of identity by the Foundation. This means, in cases of
9048	fraud or theft of keys, The Foundation can roll back scootchains,
9049	invalidating many linked transactions.
9050	(5) The system allows transactions to be performed in which
9051	ownership of the cryptographic units is changed. A transaction
9052	statement can only be issued by an entity proving the current
9053	ownership of these units.
9054	Yes, but again the ownership language is wrong. Pledges are bearers
9055	of responsibility for an idea and choose to be. They can choose not to
9056	be by trading that responsibility as allowed by the individual Scoot
9057	governance.
9058	(6) If two different instructions for changing the ownership of the
9059	same cryptographic units are simultaneously entered, the system
9060	performs at most one of them.
9061	135 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enron_scandal
9062	136 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sam_Bankman-Fried
9063	
9064	-- 256 of 335 --
9065	
9066	255
9067	Yes. This would either be a bug, or fraud. Bugs can be fixed, and since
9068	we have central authority, we can require changes in protocol. In cases
9069	of fraud, the only recourse is to banish the pledge, or in the value domain,
9070	a particular address.
9071	Since Scoot does not meet 2 out of the 6 criteria for being a
9072	cryptocurrency, I’m not sure why you believe your Exclusive
9073	Premium Crypto Validation Package would be appropriate for my
9074	purposes. I do realize the Naira is at an all-time low. A billion Naira
9075	is still beyond my budget for the moment.
9076	Are you sure you’re a member of Nigerian royalty and you don’t
9077	understand my sports references? That’s really confusing for me.
9078	As to your other question, no, I’m not worried at all, not one bit,
9079	about the future market value of my personal crypto stash. Interpret
9080	that as you would like.
9081	Happy? Six hundred sumfin word answer to your findings report,
9082	with zero sports references.
9083	Lean, clean and mean, like The Dream in a Nick Anderson
9084	nightmare.
9085	How can you claim not to understand all the basketball
9086	references when you list Steph Curry, Klay Thompson and Lebron
9087	James as clients on your resume? No, James Harden does not
9088	endorse crypto, that was a strip-club commercial. Houston is world
9089	famous for just about everything, it’s a common mistake. Ask any
9090	NBA player. Your knowledge on mid-1300 century gong craftmanship
9091	doesn’t impress me at all.
9092	As my momma would put it, your expert knowledge is special. I
9093	don’t have any more crypto to pay you, I guess if you want to
9094	continue this relationship, you’ll have to show your face and take
9095	cash. There’s a private field where you can land your Gulfstream
9096	about 20 miles from where I live. I hope you can eat spicy food, cause
9097	that’s about all we got around here, but there are a few options if
9098	you’ve got a sensitive tummy. You ever had a Whataburger?
9099	Again, thank you. My first crypto-consulting experience has been
9100	insightful.
9101	Brandon
9102	
9103	-- 257 of 335 --
9104	
9105	256

9107	Texas Prehistory

Appendix D – System Technical Description
This section was moved from the main text in the Second Edition.
FIGURE 6 - THE FOUNDATION - RESPONSIBILITY AND VALUE DOMAINS
A Scoot is formed as organization of proportional responsibility
for a legacy of genius. Each Scoot shall have a charter, clearly stating
the purpose and method of governance, which shall be enforceable
via methods in the protocol. Holders of scoot have no rights other
than the elective governance of the Scoot, but a responsibility – a
pledge – to increase the appreciation of the subject of the Scoot.
The Foundation and its members, who are called pledges, mint a
class of coin known as scoot (lowercase). An instance of scoot is
identified by an index X, as in scoot(X). Asimov is a special case of
scoot only minted by The Foundation. scoot(0) == asimov. More
precisely, scoot with index < 1 can only be minted by The Foundation.
